text: The surgeon incises the peritoneum on the right side, medial to the right ureter, at the level of the sacral promontory. The incision is deepened to reach the presacral fascia, developing a plane both inferiorly and superiorly. The avascular plane between the presacral fascia and the rectum is visible, being developed using both blunt and sharp dissection techniques. A hook dissection is performed, reaching the bottom of the sacral halo.

The surgeon continues the resection beyond the tip of the coccyx until the fibers of the pelvic diaphragm or levator muscles are visible. The resection is confirmed complete when a finger introduced digitally from below is visible through the laparoscopic view.

The surgeon uses a hook to divide the right lateral ligament during dissection, proceeding bilaterally. The dissection extends into the sacral hollow, avoiding cutting the anterior peritoneal reflection of the rectum.
The surgeon completes the dissection by dividing the right lateral attachments of the rectum, followed by similar dissection on the left lateral side, ensuring both sides are fully separated.

The surgeon incises the peritoneal reflection of the rectosigmoid at the lateral pelvic wall, using careful hook dissection to deepen the incision layer by layer. The left ureter is visible, and the dissection enters the sacral hollow, mirroring the approach taken on the right side by cutting the lateral ligaments.

After completing the dissection, the surgeon straightens the rectum and sutures it to the sacral promontory using 2-0 polypropylene sutures. Traction is applied to ensure proper alignment, and similar suturing is performed on the left side, attaching the rectum to the presacral fascia. The rectum is visibly straightened once the suturing is complete.
